title: Rustic Farmhouse Headboard (Twin)
summary: A planked twin headboard framed in trim, with an angled cross-brace for the farmhouse look. Mostly straight cuts and a nail gun.
description:
A freestanding twin headboard built the farmhouse way: a field of vertical 1x4 planks, framed all around with 1x2 and 1x3 trim to look like a solid panel, and finished with a stepped top cap and an angled cross-brace across the center. It leans against or bolts to a bed frame, so it carries no real load — which is why almost the whole thing is glue and brad nails.

The honest challenge is board width. Nominal 1x4 stock is not always exactly 3-1/2" wide, and twelve planks side by side will accumulate any error into a panel that's too wide or too narrow for its trim. Measure your actual boards, dry-lay all twelve, and adjust before you commit — or buy a couple of extra boards so you can cull the worst ones.

The cross-brace looks harder than it is: two boards cut from a 1x4 with both ends at 30° off square, parallel to each other, forming a shallow V or a single diagonal across the panel. Cut, test against the panel, and nail it on last.

tools.note: Square crosscuts and the 30° cross-brace ends.

materials.name: 1x4, 8 ft
materials.note: Panel boards, trim, cross-brace. Buy one extra to cull for width.

materials.name: 1x3, 8 ft
materials.note: Front legs, back supports, top trim.

cutList.note: Both ends 30° off square, ends parallel; 30" short-point to long-point.

steps.title: Assemble the panel
steps.body: Lay the twelve 1x4 panel boards side by side. Measure your actual board widths first — nominal 1x4 isn't always exactly 3-1/2", and small errors add up across twelve boards. Once you're happy with the width, secure the 1x3 back support boards across the panel boards with a brad nailer and glue.

steps.title: Add the side legs
steps.body: Attach the 1x2 leg sides to the sides of the panel per the diagram, keeping the outside edges flush.

steps.title: Add the front legs
steps.body: Attach the 1x3 front legs over the 1x2 sides. As you nail into the 1x2, keep the seam tight and flush so the two boards read as one solid leg rather than a stack.

steps.title: Attach the front trim
steps.body: Use 2" nails and glue to attach the front trim to the panel boards, keeping the outside edges flush.

steps.title: Add the center trim
steps.body: Mark the headboard center per the diagram. Glue and 1-1/4" finish nails hold the center trim to the panel.

steps.title: Build the stepped top cap
steps.body: Attach the 1x3 top trim across the top of the headboard, then land the 1x4 top cap on it with a 1/2" overhang on all edges. The step between the two trim widths is what gives the top its farmhouse profile.

steps.title: Cut and add the cross-brace
steps.body: It looks harder than it is. From a 1x4, cut two boards with both ends at 30° off square, ends parallel — each measures 30" from short point to long point. Fit them against the panel to form the diagonal brace, then glue and nail. Sand everything and finish; a distressed or dry-brushed paint suits the rustic look.

images.alt: Rustic planked farmhouse headboard for a twin bed with an angled cross-brace
